message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="lea-verou" color="blue" summary="Fixes done, requesting visual design review">
Hey Steve, the fixes are already done and all tests pass. Here's what changed:

**Bug 1**: `templates/index.html` lines 6-7 -- added `class="visually-hidden"` to both nav links. The hero section remains the sole visual focal point, no duplicate CTAs.

**Bug 2**: `static/css/main.css` line 772 -- added `color: var(--color-fg)` to `.todo-item__toggle` in the components layer. Uses the design token (resolves to `--color-gray-800`) so the checkbox characters are visible against the transparent background. No hardcoded values.

**Tests**: `e2e/user-journey.spec.ts` -- two new regression tests verify the visual hiding (boundingBox 1x1px check) and the toggle color (computed color != white).

Would appreciate your review from the visual design perspective -- does `var(--color-fg)` feel right for the toggle checkbox weight, or would you prefer a different token like `--color-fg-muted`?
</teammate-message>

content.text: Lea already asked me a direct question about the token choice. Let me respond to her directly.

input.content:
Great work on both fixes, Lea. Clean and minimal.

To answer your question: `var(--color-fg)` is the right choice over `--color-fg-muted`. Here's my reasoning:

The checkbox character (☐ / ☑) sits at the start of the todo item row, right next to the title text. The title uses the default `--color-fg` (gray-800). If the checkbox used `--color-fg-muted` (gray-600), there would be an awkward weight mismatch -- the checkbox would look lighter than the title it's associated with, making the row feel visually unbalanced.

The checkbox is a primary interactive element (the main action for each todo), so it should carry full visual weight. Muted color would make it feel secondary or optional, which is the wrong signal. The edit pencil and delete X use `--color-fg-muted` because they ARE secondary actions -- the toggle is not.

One more thing: for completed items, the title already gets `color: var(--color-fg-muted)` plus `text-decoration: line-through`. The checkbox staying at `--color-fg` for completed items is fine -- it's still the interactive element to toggle back, and the muted+strikethrough title provides enough visual differentiation for the completed state.

I've already sent my full approval to the team lead. No blocking concerns.
input.summary: color-fg is correct, explained design rationale

routing.summary: color-fg is correct, explained design rationale
routing.content:
Great work on both fixes, Lea. Clean and minimal.

To answer your question: `var(--color-fg)` is the right choice over `--color-fg-muted`. Here's my reasoning:

The checkbox character (☐ / ☑) sits at the start of the todo item row, right next to the title text. The title uses the default `--color-fg` (gray-800). If the checkbox used `--color-fg-muted` (gray-600), there would be an awkward weight mismatch -- the checkbox would look lighter than the title it's associated with, making the row feel visually unbalanced.

The checkbox is a primary interactive element (the main action for each todo), so it should carry full visual weight. Muted color would make it feel secondary or optional, which is the wrong signal. The edit pencil and delete X use `--color-fg-muted` because they ARE secondary actions -- the toggle is not.

One more thing: for completed items, the title already gets `color: var(--color-fg-muted)` plus `text-decoration: line-through`. The checkbox staying at `--color-fg` for completed items is fine -- it's still the interactive element to toggle back, and the muted+strikethrough title provides enough visual differentiation for the completed state.

I've already sent my full approval to the team lead. No blocking concerns.

content.text: Responded to Lea's question with design rationale for why `--color-fg` is the correct token choice. The visual weight hierarchy in the todo row should be: checkbox and title at full weight (`--color-fg`), secondary actions like edit and delete at reduced weight (`--color-fg-muted`). This matches the existing pattern in the design system.
